meta.name: New York-Style Cheesecake (Custard Precision)
meta.author: SSA Canonical Catalog
meta.description: A classic New York-style cheesecake: dense, creamy, custard-based, with a baked graham crust. This recipe is a precision example: room-temp ingredients, low mixing to avoid air, water-bath bake, and slow cooling to prevent cracks. Flour note: not flour-based except optional crust binder; fresh-milled vs commercial flour is generally not applicable.

ingredients.label: All-purpose flour or cornstarch (optional crust binder)
ingredients.notes: About 1 tbsp. Optional; helps crust hold together. Fresh-milled flour is not recommended here; use AP or cornstarch if needed.

ingredients.label: Lemon zest (optional)
ingredients.notes: About 1 tsp; brightens flavor without making it 'lemony'.

ingredients.label: Egg yolk (optional, richer set)
ingredients.notes: Optional; adds richness and slightly firmer custard.

ingredients.label: Cornstarch (optional, stabilizer)
ingredients.notes: About 1 tbsp. Optional; helps reduce cracking and improves slice. Traditionalists may omit.

steps.title: Precision setup: bring ingredients to room temperature
steps.body: For a smooth, crack-resistant cheesecake: let cream cheese, eggs, sour cream, and cream sit at room temperature 1–2 hours. Cold ingredients cause lumps and encourage overmixing (introducing air).

steps.title: Prep springform pan for water bath
steps.body: Heat oven to 325°F (163°C). Wrap the outside of a 9-inch springform pan with 2–3 layers of heavy-duty foil to prevent leaks. Set aside.

steps.title: Make and bake the crust
steps.body: Mix graham crumbs, sugar, salt, and melted butter (and optional binder). Press firmly into the bottom (and 1 inch up the sides if desired). Bake 10 minutes. Cool while you make filling.

steps.title: Beat cream cheese (low air)
steps.body: In a large bowl, beat cream cheese on low-medium just until smooth (about 1–2 minutes). Scrape bowl often. Avoid whipping air into it—air causes puffing and cracks.

steps.title: Add sugar and salt
steps.body: Add sugar and salt (and optional cornstarch) and beat on low until combined and smooth. Scrape bowl.

steps.title: Add sour cream, cream, vanilla
steps.body: Mix in sour cream, heavy cream, vanilla, and optional lemon zest on low just until uniform. Scrape bowl.

steps.title: Add eggs last (minimal mixing)
steps.body: Add eggs one at a time on low speed, mixing only until each disappears. Add optional yolk if using. Do not overmix—this is the most important anti-crack step.

steps.title: Optional: de-bubble for a flawless top
steps.body: Tap the bowl gently on the counter to release bubbles. If you see bubbles on the surface after pouring, pop with a toothpick.

steps.title: Fill the crust
steps.body: Pour filling into cooled crust. Smooth the top. Place the foil-wrapped springform pan in a large roasting pan.

steps.title: Add hot water bath
steps.body: Carefully pour hot water into the roasting pan to come about halfway up the sides of the springform pan. Water bath provides gentle, even heat and reduces cracking.

steps.title: Bake (doneness by jiggle/temperature)
steps.body: Bake 60–80 minutes. The cheesecake is done when the edges are set but the center (2–3 inches) still jiggles like gelatin when gently shaken. Optional thermometer: center 150–155°F (65–68°C) is ideal.

steps.title: Slow cool to prevent cracks
steps.body: Turn off oven, crack the door 1–2 inches, and let cheesecake cool in the oven 45–60 minutes. This prevents rapid contraction that causes cracks.

steps.title: Cool fully, then chill overnight
steps.body: Remove from water bath, unwrap foil, and cool on a rack until room temperature. Refrigerate uncovered 4 hours, then cover and chill at least 8 hours (overnight best) for full set and clean slices.

steps.title: Release and slice cleanly
steps.body: Run a thin knife around the edge, release springform. Slice with a warm, clean knife (dip in hot water and wipe between cuts).

notes: Baking precision model: room-temp ingredients + low mixing + water bath + slow cooling = smooth cheesecake with minimal cracking.
Crust notes: Graham crust is classic. For a flour-based crust, use a cookie/biscuit crust made from your bakery catalog (fresh-milled or commercial flour cookies).
Cracks are cosmetic: if cracks occur, cover with sour cream topping or fruit compote.
Do not overbake: a fully solid center in the oven usually means an overbaked cheesecake that will be dry or crack as it cools.

storage.freezer: Freeze slices or whole cheesecake (well-wrapped) up to 2 months. Thaw overnight in refrigerator.
